title: Half- and Full-Page Scrolling
subtitle: {key:Ctrl-D} / {key:Ctrl-U} / {key:Ctrl-F} / {key:Ctrl-B} — scroll without losing place.
summary: Ctrl-D scrolls down half a page; Ctrl-U scrolls up half. Ctrl-F is forward a full page; Ctrl-B is back a page.

blocks.text: Two ways to fly through a file: half-page and full-page scrolls. Half-page is gentler — your cursor and the surrounding context move together. Full-page is faster and good for jumping past large regions you don't care about.

blocks.title: These Move the Cursor *and* the Viewport
blocks.text:
Vim doesn't have a separate scroll position from the cursor. The viewport's top line is just `getline(1) … (top + winheight - 1)`. When you scroll, both the *visible top line* and the *cursor* move. The two stay in step so the cursor never goes off-screen.

The distance for {key:Ctrl-D}/{key:Ctrl-U} comes from the `'scroll'` option (default: half the window height). You can change it with `:set scroll=10` or with a count: {key:1}{key:0}{key:Ctrl-D} sets `'scroll'` to 10 *and* scrolls 10. So the count is sticky for next time.

{key:Ctrl-F}/{key:Ctrl-B} are full page. They're slightly less smooth than the half-page ones because the cursor and visible region jump farther in one keystroke.
If your terminal eats {key:Ctrl-F} (some shells use it for forward-search history), try the alternative bindings or remap. {key:Ctrl-D}/{key:Ctrl-U} are nearly always free.
